items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).

items.q: Most movements in the human body use which class of lever?
items.why: Students assume the most familiar (first class - seesaw) is most common. Actually most body levers are third class (effort between fulcrum and load) - like the biceps curl. They sacrifice mechanical advantage for speed/range.

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: How many players in a netball team?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 7. “11” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Basketball. “Netball” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many players in a basketball team on court?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
